1203011.assessment: CRITICAL: Spectroscopic z=2.98 QSO at 0.13" separation. Our photo-z=8.24 is WRONG. This is a known z~3 quasar, not a z~8 galaxy.

1030374.assessment: KNOWN submillimeter galaxy with 16 papers. Photo-z=5.818 from literature. Our z=11.9 estimate disagrees strongly. Likely a dusty galaxy at z~5-6, not z~12. Dust reddening mimics high-z dropout.

1125125.assessment: KNOWN radio galaxy with 8 papers. Photo-z=7.96 from literature (vs our z=11.88). Some agreement on high-z nature but our z is much higher. Also radio-detected = possible AGN contamination.

conclusion: All three top candidates are known objects with existing SIMBAD entries and literature. None are new discoveries. Two have spectroscopic/photometric redshifts significantly different from our estimates.

1203011.explanation: QSO SED can mimic Lyman break at z~8. Known problem in photometric surveys.

1030374.verdict: KNOWN — AzGN 31, dusty SMG, likely z~5-6
1030374.our_photoz_status: likely wrong (11.9 vs 5.8) — dust mimics dropout
1030374.explanation: Submillimeter galaxy = dusty. Dust reddening mimics Lyman break dropout. 16 papers.

1125125.explanation: Radio-detected at z~8 would be very unusual. Already studied in 8 papers. Not new.

bottom_line: All three top candidates are known objects in astronomical databases. None qualify as new discoveries. The systematic kill pipeline (Phase 9) successfully prevented false discovery claims.
